React Interview Prep
💡 Read this out to ace your Frontend Interviews
Important Topics
Render and Re-render
Mental Model: Mind Map
Render in React
│
├── 1. Initial Render (Mount)
│ 	├── First time component is inserted into the D
OM
│ 	├── useEffect(() => {}, []) runs after this
│ 	└── useLayoutEffect runs right before browser p
aints
│
├── 2. Re-render (Update)
│ 	├── Happens when:
│ 	│ 	├── state changes via useState/useReducer

│ 	│ 	└──

ducer

│ 	│ 	└── context value changes
│ 	├── React re-executes component function

│ 	└── Decides what to update in the real DOM (rec
onciliation)
│
├── 3. React.memo + useCallback/useMemo
│ 	├── Helps prevent unnecessary re-execution or c
hild updates
│

unmount)
└── New effect runs after the re-render DOM is
committed
Initial Render

nder (or

committed
Initial Render Mount Phase):
When a component is first added to the UI
React calls your component function ( App() , Button() , etc.)
Returns JSX (or React.createElement )
React builds a virtual DOM tree
It renders to the real DOM
Triggers effects:
useEffect(() => {
console.log("Mounted");
} , []);
Re-render Update Phase):
This is the core of React's "reactive" model.
React Interview Prep	2

-- 2 of 95 --

A re-render happens when:

eact's "reactive" model.
React Interview Prep	2

-- 2 of 95 --

A re-render happens when:
Triggered By 	Example
useState change 	setCount(1)
useReducer dispatch 	dispatch({ type: 'ADD' })
Props from parent change 	App updates → child props change
Context value update 	Provider value changes
What happens:
React re-invokes the component function
Gets new JSX
Builds a new virtual DOM
Diffs it with the previous virtual DOM
Updates only the changed parts in the real DOM
Thatʼs called reconciliation
React.memo, useMemo,

only the changed parts in the real DOM
Thatʼs called reconciliation
React.memo, useMemo, useCallback:
We will use these in detail after it but for now understand why these are
used and why these are imp in rendering
These help optimize unnecessary re-renders by caching:
React.memo → skips re-rendering if props havenʼt changed
useMemo → skips recalculating expensive values
useCallback → skips recreating identical function references
These do not prevent re-renders — they help avoid unnecessary re-
renders.
Cleanup

riggers re-render
JSX re-evaluated
Cleanup prints: "Cleanup runs"
Then new effect prints: "Effect runs"
React Hooks
useState
useState lets functional components hold & Manage the local
Component.
Before hooks, only class components could hold state with this.state .
Now, with useState , you can have local state in any functional
component.
Basic Syntax:
const [state , setState] = useState(initial state);
Example
import { useState } from 'react';
function Counter() {
const [count, setCount] = useState(0); // count

tate } from 'react'; function Counter() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); // count is state variable return (
React Interview Prep 5 -- 5 of 95 --

{count}

); } Each time setCount() is called, the component re-renders with the new value. How useState Works Under the Hood When you call useState , React does the following: Creates an internal memory slot (in the Fiber node) Stores the initialValue there Returns two things:

ernal memory slot (in the Fiber node)
 Stores the initialValue there
 Returns two things:
The current value from memory
A setState function that schedules a re-render
On each render, React matches the hooks by order, not by name.
React Internally Stores Hooks Like an Array
function Conponent(){
const [a] =useState(1); //hooks[0]
const [b] = useState(2) //hooks[0]
}
Internally it tracks like :
hooks = [1, 2]
Thatʼs why you canʼt call hooks inside if/else or loops — React must
know the order of hooks each render.

anʼt call hooks inside if/else or loops — React must
know the order of hooks each render.
React Interview Prep	6

-- 6 of 95 --

Does setState replace or merge the value?
useState replaces the value.
Unlike class components ( this.setState ) which merge, useState
replaces the value entirely
const [user, setUser] = useState({ name: 'John', age:
25 });
setUser({ name: 'Doe' }); // age is gone!
To preserve age, you must do:
setUser(prev => ({ ...prev, name: 'Doe' }));
Functional Updates (based on previous state)
If

User(prev => ({ ...prev, name: 'Doe' }));
Functional Updates (based on previous state)
If your update depends on previous value, do this:
setCount(prev => prev + 1);
This is safe, especially with concurrent rendering.
Common Pitfalls
Mistake 	Why it breaks
Calling useState conditionally 	Breaks hook ordering
Not copying old state in object/array
updates Youʼll lose previous data
Setting state and expecting it to update
instantly
Updates are asynchronous and
batched
⏱ Is setState synchronous?
React Interview Prep	7

ly
Updates are asynchronous and
batched
⏱ Is setState synchronous?
React Interview Prep	7

-- 7 of 95 --

❌ No.
React schedules the state update. The new state is applied on the
next render.
Thatʼs why:
setCount(1);
console.log(count); // still shows previous value
Want the latest value? Use useEffect or the updater form:
setCount(prev => {
console.log(prev);
return prev + 1;
});
Real-world use cases
Form inputs ( useState("") )
Toggling modal open/close
Page tabs ( useState("home") )
Simple UI flags (loading,

one of Reactʼs most powerful hooks for avoiding prop

}, []);
useContext
one of Reactʼs most powerful hooks for avoiding prop drilling and
sharing global state.
we can Define the useContext that lets you consume or use the context
data in a functional component
Think of useContext as a portal into shared global data — like a shortcut
to avoid passing props through every level of a component tree.
Why useContext ?
<App>
└── <Dashboard>
└── <Sidebar>
└── <UserProfile>
└── <Avatar name={user.name} />
If you want to pass the userʼs name to <Avatar> ,

rofile>
└── <Avatar name={user.name} />
If you want to pass the userʼs name to <Avatar> , you'd have to prop drill
from the top — passing it through multiple layers of components that
donʼt even care about it.
This is annoying
Hard to maintain
Makes refactoring dangerous
Solution: Create a Context with the user, and use useContext wherever
needed.
How to Use useContext
1 Create the Context:
const UserContext = React.createContext();
React Interview Prep	12

-- 12 of 95 --

2 Provide the Context
Wrap the portion of

?
When you use a context:
const value = useContext(SomeContext);
React does 2 things:
 It searches upward in the component tree for the nearest matching
<SomeContext.Provider />
 It retrieves the value from that provider and keeps it in sync
React Fiber ensures that any component consuming context will re-
render when the context value changes.
React Interview Prep	13

-- 13 of 95 --

What Causes Re-renders?
This is critical to understand as an Engineer.
Every time the value prop of the <Context.Provider> changes:

o understand as an Engineer.
Every time the value prop of the <Context.Provider> changes:
All components that use useContext(MyContext) will re-render
Even if the new value is identical (e.g., same object, but a new
reference)
Example:
<UserContext.Provider value={{ name: 'Moiz' }}> // n
ew object every render
This causes unnecessary re-renders
Fix:
const user = useMemo(() => ({ name: 'Moiz' }), []);
<UserContext.Provider value={user}>
Question/Answers:
What happens if you change the value passed to a Context

value={user}>
Question/Answers:
What happens if you change the value passed to a Context Provider
on every render?
How does React track which context value to give a consumer?
Why do we say useContext causes a re-render?
How would you optimize a large object passed via context?
useRef
useRef gives you a persistent reference object whose .current property
does not cause re-renders when it changes.
Itʼs like a tiny box that React doesnʼt care about. You can put any value
inside — a DOM node, a number, a function,

eact doesnʼt care about. You can put any value
inside — a DOM node, a number, a function, or even a component —
and update it without affecting rendering.
React Interview Prep	14

-- 14 of 95 --

Syntax:
const ref = useRef(initialValue);
The returned ref object has the shape:
{ current: initialValue }
useReducer
We say it Mini Redux
Why useReducer?
When your componentʼs state:
Involves multiple values
Has dependent updates
Is updated in different ways based on user actions
Using multiple useState() calls becomes

s updated in different ways based on user actions
Using multiple useState() calls becomes hard to manage and maintain.
What Is a Reducer?
Think of useReducer as a mailbox system:
State = mailbox contents
Action = mail request (what user wants to do)
Reducer = mail clerk (decides what to do based on act
ion)
A reducer is a pure function:
function reducer(state , action){
return newState;
}
MIND MAP: useReducer Hook:
useReducer
│

│ 	├── Manage complex state logic

tialState)

nable

│ 	├── Shared logic via context
│
└── Bonus

└── Avoids prop drilling like Redux
Basic Syntax:
const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducerFn, initi
alState);

switch (action.type) {
case 'TOGGLE':
return { on: !state.on };

useLayoutEffect
useLayoutEffect is just like useEffect , but it runs synchronously
after
DOM mutations and
before the browser paints the screen.
So if you need to:
Measure DOM layout ( offsetHeight , etc.)
Make immediate visual updates before the screen repaints
Avoid flickers or visual glitches
Then useLayoutEffect is your tool.

the screen repaints
Avoid flickers or visual glitches
Then useLayoutEffect is your tool.
Mind Map:
useLayoutEffect
│
├── Timing
│ 	├── Runs after DOM is updated
│ 	└── Before browser paints (blocks paint)
│
├── Use Cases
React Interview Prep	19

│ 	├── DOM measurement (e.g., offsetWidth)

│ 	├── Fixing layout glitches
│ 	└── Avoid flicker before paint
│
├── Difference from useEffect
│ 	├── useEffect = async → after paint
│ 	└── useLayoutEffect = sync → before

ʼt impact layout
Donʼt use it everywhere instead of useEffect

These questions have been collected from top engineers working at various
companies. By thoroughly understanding the answers, you can gain a solid grasp
of React.
I have divided the questions into three parts for better clarity and structure:

Difference Between Virtual DOM and Real DOM
General Understanding:
Real DOM: The actual Document Object Model that the browser uses to
render the web page. Itʼs a tree structure of HTML elements.
Virtual DOM: A lightweight, in-memory copy of the Real DOM that
React uses to optimize updates. React

A lightweight, in-memory copy of the Real DOM that
React uses to optimize updates. React updates the Virtual DOM first,
compares it with the previous version (reconciliation), and applies only
the necessary changes to the Real DOM.
Real-Life Example: Imagine the Real DOM as a physical whiteboard you
update directly—slow and costly. The Virtual DOM is like a sketchpad
React Interview Prep	22

-- 22 of 95 --

where you draft changes first, then efficiently update the whiteboard.
Coding Example: You donʼt directly

improve
performance compared to direct Real DOM manipulation?"
Answer: It batches updates and calculates the minimal changes,
reducing expensive DOM operations.
Why/When to Use: Use the Virtual DOM (via React) when building dynamic
UIs to improve performance by minimizing direct DOM manipulation.
2. What is JSX?
General Understanding: JSX JavaScript XML) is a syntax extension for
JavaScript that lets you write HTML-like code within JavaScript. In React,
itʼs used to define UI components.
Real-Life Example: Writing

ithin JavaScript. In React, itʼs used to define UI components. Real-Life Example: Writing instead of React.createElement('h1', null, 'Hello') makes code more intuitive, like drafting a webpage layout. Coding Example: const element =

Hello, world!

; Interview-Style Question: "What happens to JSX code under the hood?" React Interview Prep 23 -- 23 of 95 -- Answer: Itʼs transpiled by Babel into React.createElement calls, which create React elements. Why/When to Use: Use JSX for readable,

.createElement calls, which
create React elements.
Why/When to Use: Use JSX for readable, declarative UI code in React,
especially when working with complex component structures.
3. Describe Events in React.js
General Understanding: React uses synthetic events, which wrap native
browser events for consistent behavior across platforms. Event handlers
are attached using camelCase (e.g., onClick).
Real-Life Example: A button that triggers a form submission when clicked.
Coding Example:
function Button() {
const

n that triggers a form submission when clicked.
Coding Example:
function Button() {
const handleClick = () => alert('Clicked!');
return <button onClick={handleClick}>Click Me</button
>;
}
Interview-Style Question: "How do synthetic events differ from native
events?"
Answer: Synthetic events normalize event properties and behavior
across browsers, pooling events for performance.
Why/When to Use: Use React events for handling user interactions like
clicks or inputs in a consistent, browser-agnostic way.
4. What are

ser interactions like
clicks or inputs in a consistent, browser-agnostic way.
4. What are Props?
General Understanding: Props (properties) are read-only data passed from
a parent to a child component in React.
Real-Life Example: A reusable Button component that displays different
text based on a label prop, like "Submit" or "Cancel."
Coding Example:
React Interview Prep	24

-- 24 of 95 --

function Button(props) {
return <button>{props.label}</button>;
}
<Button label="Submit" />
Interview-Style Question: "Why are

on>{props.label}</button>;
}
<Button label="Submit" />
Interview-Style Question: "Why are props immutable in a child
component?"
Answer: To enforce a unidirectional data flow and prevent unintended
side effects.
Why/When to Use: Use props to pass dynamic data or callbacks, making
components reusable and modular.
5. Define State
General Understanding: State is a JavaScript object that holds data that
can change over time within a component, triggering re-renders when
updated.
Real-Life Example: A counter that

--

Answer: Class components use this.state and this.setState; functional
components use the useState hook.
Why/When to Use: Use state to manage dynamic, component-specific
data that affects the UI.
6. Define Hooks
General Understanding: Hooks are functions that let functional
components use state and other React features (e.g., useState, useEffect),
introduced in React 16.8.
Real-Life Example: Using useState to track form input values in real-time.
Coding Example:
function Form() {
const [name, setName] =

form input values in real-time.
Coding Example:
function Form() {
const [name, setName] = useState('');
return <input value={name} onChange={(e) => setName
(e.target.value)} />;
}
Interview-Style Question: "What are the rules of hooks?"
Answer: Call hooks only at the top level (not in loops/conditions) and
only in functional components.
Why/When to Use: Use hooks to add state and lifecycle features to
functional components, avoiding class complexity.

Interview-Style Question: "Why is the key prop important?"
Answer: It helps React identify which items changed, optimizing re-
rendering.
Why/When to Use: Use lists to display dynamic collections of data
efficiently.
9. Explain Redux
General Understanding: Redux is a state management library for
predictable state updates, using a single store, actions, and reducers.
Real-Life Example: Managing a shopping cartʼs state across an e-
commerce app.
Coding Example: Basic Redux setup

e: Use Redux for large apps with complex, shared state.
10. Explain Flux Concept in React
General Understanding: Flux is an architecture pattern with unidirectional
data flow: actions → dispatcher → stores → views.
Real-Life Example: A chat app where messages update all UI parts
consistently.
React Interview Prep	28

-- 28 of 95 --

Coding Example: Flux is conceptual; Redux is a common implementation.
Interview-Style Question: "How does Flux differ from Redux?"
Answer: Flux uses multiple stores and a dispatcher;

n: "How does Flux differ from Redux?"
Answer: Flux uses multiple stores and a dispatcher; Redux uses one
store and reducers.
Why/When to Use: Use Flux (or Redux) for structured state management in
large apps.
11. What is a Higher-Order Component (HOC) in React?
General Understanding: A HOC is a function that takes a component and
returns an enhanced version with added props or behavior.
Real-Life Example: Adding a loading spinner to any component.
Coding Example:
function withLoading(Component) {
return function

like authentication. 12. What is a Presentational Component (PC)? General Understanding: Presentational components focus on UI, receiving data via props without managing state. {title}

Interview-Style Question: "How does yield relate to async operations?"
Answer: Itʼs foundational for async generators and was used pre-

14. How Do You Create Forms in React?
General Understanding: Forms use controlled components, where state
manages input values.
Real-Life Example: A signup form with

olled components, where state
manages input values.
Real-Life Example: A signup form with email and password fields.
Coding Example:
function SignupForm() {
const [email, setEmail] = useState('');
return <input value={email} onChange={(e) => setEmail
(e.target.value)} />;
}
Interview-Style Question: "Whatʼs the benefit of controlled components?"
Answer: They offer precise control over form data and validation.
Why/When to Use: Use controlled forms for validation and dynamic
behavior.
15. Explain the Concept of

Use: Use controlled forms for validation and dynamic
behavior.
15. Explain the Concept of Lifting State Up in React
General Understanding: Moving state to a parent component to share it
among children.
Real-Life Example: A form where two inputs (e.g., Celsius/Fahrenheit) sync
via shared state.
Coding Example:
function Parent() {
const [value, setValue] = useState('');
return (
<>
<Child value={value} onChange={setValue} />
<Child value={value} onChange={setValue} />

);

Interview-Style Question: "When would you lift state up?"
Answer: When multiple components need to share and update the
same data.
Why/When to Use: Use to maintain a single source of truth across
components.
16. What is the Purpose of render() in React?
General Understanding: render() in class components returns the JSX
describing the UI.
Real-Life Example: Rendering a user profile card in a class component.
Coding Example:

olledconst ref = useRef();
<input ref={ref} />;
Interview-Style Question: "When would you use uncontrolled
components?"
Answer: For simplicity or integrating with non-React libraries.
Why/When to Use: Controlled for control; uncontrolled for simplicity.
18. What Do You Understand by “Single Source of Truth”?
General Understanding: Storing state in one place to ensure consistency
and ease of management.
Real-Life Example: Using Redux to manage app-wide user settings.
Coding Example: See Redux example above.

e: Using Redux to manage app-wide user settings.
Coding Example: See Redux example above.
Interview-Style Question: "How does React implement this principle?"
Answer: Via context, Redux, or lifting state up.
Why/When to Use: Use to avoid state duplication and debugging issues.
19. How Do You Handle Routing in a React Application?
General Understanding: Routing uses libraries like React Router to
navigate between components based on URLs.
Real-Life Example: Switching between home and profile pages.
Coding Example:

sed on URLs.
Real-Life Example: Switching between home and profile pages.
Coding Example:

import { BrowserRouter, Route, Switch } from 'react-router
-dom';
<BrowserRouter>
<Switch>
<Route path="/" exact component={Home} />

</Switch>
</BrowserRouter>
Interview-Style Question: "Whatʼs the difference between client-side and
server-side routing?"
Answer: Client-side handles routes in the browser; server-side requires
server

routing?"
Answer: Client-side handles routes in the browser; server-side requires
server requests.
Why/When to Use: Use for SPAs to manage navigation without full page
reloads.
20. Advantages of React
General Understanding:
Component-based architecture for reusability.
Virtual DOM for performance.
Rich ecosystem and community.
Easy to build complex UIs.
Real-Life Example: Building a dashboard with reusable widgets.
Interview-Style Question: "How does React compare to Vue?"
Answer: React offers more flexibility

ew-Style Question: "How does React compare to Vue?"
Answer: React offers more flexibility and a larger ecosystem; Vue is
simpler out-of-the-box.
Why/When to Use: Use React for scalable, interactive UIs with a strong
community.

1. How Does React Render?
General Understanding:
React renders by maintaining a virtual DOM—a lightweight, in-memory
representation of the real DOM. When a componentʼs state or props change,
React Interview Prep	35

-- 35 of 95 --

React creates a new virtual DOM tree, compares it with the previous one

-- 35 of 95 --

React creates a new virtual DOM tree, compares it with the previous one (a
process called reconciliation), and updates only the parts of the real DOM that
have changed. This makes rendering efficient by minimizing direct DOM
manipulations, which are slow.
Real-Life Example:
Imagine the real DOM as a physical whiteboard where erasing and rewriting is
time-consuming. The virtual DOM is like a sketchpad: you draft updates there
first, then apply only the necessary changes to the whiteboard, saving

raft updates there
first, then apply only the necessary changes to the whiteboard, saving time
and effort.
Coding Example:
Hereʼs a simple component where a state change triggers a re-render:
import { useState } from 'react';function Counter() {
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
return <button onClick={() => setCount(count + 1)}>{coun
t}</button>;
}
Each click updates count, prompting React to re-render the component by
updating the virtual DOM and patching the real DOM.
Interview-Style Question:
"Explain the

Interview-Style Question:
"Explain the reconciliation process in React."
Answer: Reconciliation is where React diffs the new virtual DOM against the
old one using a heuristic algorithm, identifying changes, and applying minimal
updates to the real DOM for efficiency.
Why/When to Use:
Reactʼs rendering is automatic and optimized, so you donʼt manage it directly.
Focus on designing components and managing state/props—React handles the
rendering. Use this mechanism

gning components and managing state/props—React handles the
rendering. Use this mechanism whenever you build dynamic UIs that respond
to data changes.

2. What is the Cleanup Function in useEffect?
General Understanding:
The cleanup function in useEffect is an optional function returned from the
effect callback. It runs when the component unmounts or before the effect re-
runs due to dependency changes, allowing you to clean up resources like
subscriptions or timers.

hen to Use:
Use the cleanup function whenever an effect creates a resource (e.g., timers,
subscriptions, event listeners) that needs to be released when the component
unmounts or updates.
React Interview Prep	37

-- 37 of 95 --

3. What Should Be in the Dependency Array in useEffect?
General Understanding:
The dependency array in useEffect lists variables from the componentʼs scope
that the effect relies on. React re-runs the effect when any dependency
changes, ensuring the effect stays synchronized with the

s the effect when any dependency
changes, ensuring the effect stays synchronized with the latest values.
Real-Life Example:
If an effect updates the page title based on a count state, include count in the
array so the title updates whenever count changes.

Updater() {
const [count, setCount] = useState(0); useEffect(() =>
{
document.title = `Count: ${count}`; }, [count]); // R
e-runs when count changes return <button onClick={() => s

t: ${count}`; }, [count]); // R
e-runs when count changes return <button onClick={() => s
etCount(count + 1)}>{count}</button>;
}
Interview-Style Question:
"What happens if you omit a dependency from the array?"
Answer: The effect might use outdated (stale) values, causing bugs or
inconsistent behavior, as it wonʼt re-run when that value changes.
Why/When to Use:
Always include all dependencies (state, props, or variables) the effect uses to
keep it consistent with the componentʼs current data. Omit the array

) the effect uses to
keep it consistent with the componentʼs current data. Omit the array entirely for
effects that run on every render.

useCallback: Memoizes a function, preventing it from being recreated on
every render unless its dependencies change.

computation until its dependencies change.

5. SyntheticEvent in React
General Understanding:
SyntheticEvent is Reactʼs cross-browser wrapper around native DOM events. It
provides a consistent API for event handling, abstracting browser differences
and optimizing performance via event

}
Interview-Style Question:
"Why does React use SyntheticEvent instead of native events?"
Answer: It ensures consistent behavior across browsers and improves
performance by pooling events and delegating them at the root level.
Why/When to Use:
You use SyntheticEvent implicitly with Reactʼs event handlers (onClick,
onChange, etc.). Itʼs always there when handling events—no need to configure
it.

useRef over useState?"

7. Can you explain the React lifecycle?
React Interview Prep	41

-- 41 of 95 --

General Understanding:
The React component lifecycle refers to the series of phases a component
goes through from creation to removal. These phases allow

he series of phases a component
goes through from creation to removal. These phases allow developers to hook
into specific moments to execute code, such as initializing state, fetching data,
or cleaning up resources. The lifecycle is divided into three main phases:
 Mounting: When a component is created and inserted into the DOM.
 Updating: When a component re-renders due to changes in state or props.
 Unmounting: When a component is removed from the DOM.
Each phase has specific methods (in class components) that

onent is removed from the DOM.
Each phase has specific methods (in class components) that let you control
behavior. Functional components achieve similar functionality using hooks like
useEffect.
Mounting Phase
constructor(): Initializes state and binds methods. Called before the
component is mounted.
static getDerivedStateFromProps(props, state): A static method to update
state based on initial props (rarely used).
render(): Returns the JSX to describe the UI.
componentDidMount(): Runs after the component is

r(): Returns the JSX to describe the UI.
componentDidMount(): Runs after the component is added to the DOM,
ideal for fetching data or setting up subscriptions.
Updating Phase
static getDerivedStateFromProps(props, state): Updates state based on
new props (same as in mounting).
shouldComponentUpdate(next Props, next State): Determines if the
component should re-render (for performance optimization).
render(): Re-renders the component with updated state/props.
getSnapshotBeforeUpdate(prepregs, prevState): Captures

omponent with updated state/props.
getSnapshotBeforeUpdate(prepregs, prevState): Captures information
(e.g., scroll position) before DOM updates (rarely used).
React Interview Prep	42

-- 42 of 95 --

componentDidUpdate(prepregs, prevState, snapshot): Runs after
updates are applied, useful for side effects like DOM updates or additional
data fetching.
Unmounting Phase
componentWillUnmount(): Runs before the component is removed, used
for cleanup (e.g., clearing timers, canceling API calls).
Functional Components

ved, used
for cleanup (e.g., clearing timers, canceling API calls).
Functional Components with Hooks
In functional components, the useEffect hook replaces most lifecycle methods,
combining componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, and
componentWillUnmount functionality. The useLayoutEffect hook can also
mimic getSnapshotBeforeUpdate in specific cases.
Real-Life Example:
Imagine a chat application:
Mounting: When the chat window component loads, it fetches initial
messages (componentDidMount) and sets up a WebSocket

component loads, it fetches initial
messages (componentDidMount) and sets up a WebSocket to listen for
new messages.
Updating: When a new message arrives (state update), the component re-
renders to display it, and componentDidUpdate scrolls the chat to the
bottom.
Unmounting: When the user closes the chat, componentWillUnmount
disconnects the WebSocket to avoid memory leaks.
This lifecycle ensures the component initializes correctly, updates efficiently,
and cleans up properly.

side effects like updating the DOM (e.g., scrolling a chat window) or

Unmounting: Clean up resources. Use componentWillUnmount to remove

In functional components, useEffect combines these behaviors, with the

Why/When to Use the Lifecycle
Why:
The lifecycle methods (or useEffect in functional components) give you precise
control over a componentʼs behavior at different stages, ensuring proper
initialization, updates, and cleanup. This is critical for managing side effects,
optimizing performance, and preventing issues like memory leaks.
When to Use:
Mounting: Use componentDidMount or useEffect with an empty
dependency array for one-time setup, like fetching data or

unt or useEffect with an empty
dependency array for one-time setup, like fetching data or initializing third-
party libraries.
Updating: Use componentDidUpdate or useEffect with dependencies to
respond to state/prop changes, such as updating a chart when data
React Interview Prep	47

changes.
Unmounting: Use componentWillUnmount or useEffect cleanup function to
release resources, like clearing timers or unsubscribing from APIs.
Optimization: Use shouldComponentUpdate or React.memo to prevent

unsubscribing from APIs.
Optimization: Use shouldComponentUpdate or React.memo to prevent
unnecessary re-renders, especially in performance-critical apps.
Additional Notes
Deprecated Methods: Older lifecycle methods like componentWillMount,
componentWillReceiveProps, and componentWillUpdate are deprecated in
newer React versions due to potential misuse and the introduction of safer
alternatives like getDerivedStateFromProps. Avoid using them in modern
React code.
Functional Components: Since functional components

Avoid using them in modern
React code.
Functional Components: Since functional components with hooks
(especially useEffect) are now the standard in React, prefer them over
class components unless maintaining legacy code. Hooks simplify lifecycle
management and reduce boilerplate.
Performance Considerations: Be cautious with lifecycle methods that can
trigger frequent updates (e.g., componentDidUpdate). Use dependency
arrays in useEffect or shouldComponentUpdate to control re-renders.

8. Props Drilling
General Understanding:
Props drilling occurs when you pass props through multiple component layers,
even if intermediate components donʼt use them, leading to cluttered code.
Real-Life Example:
Passing a theme prop from the app root through several components to a
deeply nested button.
Coding Example:
function App() {
return <Parent theme="dark" />;
}

trees—switch to Context or global state when it hinders maintainability.
9. Why States Need to Be Mentioned at the Top?
General Understanding:
React requires state (and hooks) to be declared at the top level of functional
components—not inside conditions, loops, or nested functions—to ensure
consistent hook calls across renders.
Real-Life Example:
Declaring useState inside an if would disrupt Reactʼs ability to track state,
causing errors.
Coding Example:

function

o track state,
causing errors.

top return <button onClick={() => setCount(count + 1)}>{c
ount}</button>;
}

Interview-Style Question:
"What happens if you declare a state inside a loop or condition?"
Answer: It breaks Reactʼs hook order, leading to runtime errors or
unpredictable behavior.
Why/When to Use:
Always declare state and hooks at the top

ors or
unpredictable behavior.
Why/When to Use:
Always declare state and hooks at the top to comply with Reactʼs rules,

10. React.StrictMode – Purpose
General Understanding:
React.StrictMode is a development tool that wraps your app or components,
enabling extra checks and warnings for potential issues like deprecated APIs
or unsafe practices.
Real-Life Example:
Wrapping your app in StrictMode during development to catch double renders
or legacy lifecycle usage.

11. Global State Management
General Understanding:
Global state management involves sharing state across multiple, unrelated
components using tools like React Context, Redux, or Zustand, avoiding props
drilling.
Real-Life Example:
Storing a userʼs login status globally so any component (header, profile, etc.)
can access it.

12. Why We Use Thunk in Redux
General Understanding:
Redux Thunk is a middleware that lets action creators return functions instead
of plain objects, enabling asynchronous logic like API calls in Redux.
